message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) is a pandemic with no specific drugs and high mortality. The most urgent thing is to find effective treatments.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine whether hydroxychloroquine application may be associated with a decreased risk of death in critically ill COVID-19 patients and what is potential mechanism.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design, Setting and Patients</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This retrospective study included all 568 critically ill COVID-19 patients who were confirmed by pathogen laboratory tests despite antiviral treatment and had severe acute respiratory distress syndrome, PAO2/FIO2 <300 with need of mechanical ventilation in Tongji Hospital, Wuhan, between February 1 of 2020 to April 8 of 2020. All 568 patients received comparable basic treatments including antiviral drugs and antibiotics, and 48 of them additionally received oral hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) treatment (200 mg twice a day for 7-10 days). Primary endpoint is mortality of patients, and inflammatory cytokines levels were compared between hydroxychloroquine and non-hydroxychloroquine (NHCQ) treatments.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In-hospital death and hospital stay time (day) were obtained, level of inflammatory cytokine (IL-6) was measured and compared between HCQ and NHCQ treatments.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>RESULTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The median age of 568 critically ill patients is 68 (57, 76) years old with 37.0% being female. Mortalities are 18.8% (9/48) in HCQ group and 45.8% (238/520) in NHCQ group (p<0.001). The time of hospital stay before patient death is 15 (10-21) days and 8 (4 - 14) days for the HCQ and NHCQ groups, respectively (p<0.05). The level of inflammatory cytokine IL-6 was significantly lowered from 22.2 (8.3-118.9) pg/mL at the beginning of the treatment to 5.2 (3.0-23.4) pg/ml (p<0.05) at the end of the treatment in the HCQ group but there is no change in the NHCQ group.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Hydroxychloroquine treatment is significantly associated with a decreased mortality in critically ill patients with COVID-19 through attenuation of inflammatory cytokine storm. Therefore, hydroxychloroquine should be prescribed for treatment of critically ill COVID-19 patients to save lives.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key Points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Could hydroxychloroquine administration be beneficial in the treatment of critically ill patients with COVID-19?</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>In this retrospective study, a total of 568 critically ill patients with COVID-19 all received basic therapy and additionally 48 of them received hydroxychloroquine for 7–10 days (200 mg twice per day). Hydroxychloroquine treatment is significantly associated with a decreased mortality in critically ill COVID-19 patients and attenuated inflammatory cytokine IL-6 level.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Our data suggest that hydroxychloroquine could be used to treat critically ill patients with COVID-19 which may save a lot of lives.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Hydroxychloroquine application is associated with a decreased mortality in critically ill patients with COVID-19

reference.unstructured: China NHCo. Chinese management guideline for COVID-19 (version 5.0). 2020; http://www.nhcgovcn/yzygj/s7653p/202002/d4b895337e19445f8d728fcaf1e3e13a.shtml.
